'''Krisnan Nevada Inu''' (born 17 March 1987) is a professional [[rugby league]] coach who is the former head Coach of the [[North Wales Crusaders]] in the [[RFL Championship]], and a former professional [[rugby league]] footballer.

He has played for both [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] and [[Samoa national rugby league team|Samoa]] at international level.

He has previously played the [[Parramatta Eels]], [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] and the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]], and the [[Catalans Dragons]] and the [[Widnes Vikings]] in the [[Super League]]. Inu is one of two players who have lost grand finals with three clubs.<ref name="foxsports.com.au">{{Cite web|url=https://www.foxsports.com.au/nrl/nrl-premiership/krisnan-inu-becomes-just-second-player-in-history-to-lose-three-nrl-grand-finals-with-three-clubs/news-story/cfcf00adb832ab7be3bf94eedcddbda6?sv=c4c114a9a966239560d23b5cb437fce2|title = Krisnan Inu becomes just second player in history to lose three NRL grand finals with three clubs|date = 30 September 2012}}</ref> He was a member of the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup|2008 World Cup]]-winning New Zealand squad.<ref name="Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org"/>

Inu has also played [[rugby union]] for [[Stade Français]] of the [[Top 14]] competition in France.

==Background==
Inu was born in [[Auckland]], New Zealand, and is of [[Samoans|Samoan]] descent.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rugby-league.com/article/36680/leigh-sign-krisnan-inu|title=Leigh signs Krisnan Inu|website=rugby-league.com|date=22 February 2022}}</ref>

==Playing career==
===Parramatta Eels===
A [[Parramatta Eels]] junior, Inu made his first grade debut in the [[2007 NRL season]] against the [[Canberra Raiders]]. After just one first grade match he was named to play for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in the [[ANZAC Day Test]] against [[Australian Kangaroos|Australia]] at [[Suncorp Stadium]] in [[Brisbane]]. The Kangaroos ran out easy 30-6 winners over the Kiwis. After the test, Inu was dropped back to Premier League but returned to first grade through a series of injuries to regular players. Inu took on the kicking duties as Parramatta's primary kicker and full-back, [[Luke Burt]], was injured mid-season. In round 25 Inu scored three tries and kicked seven goals in Parramatta's 46-point win over Brisbane.<ref>{{cite web|title=Parramatta 68 v Brisbane 22|url=http://stats.rleague.com/rl/scorers/games/2007/200709020418.html|work=Rugby League Tables|publisher=stats.rleague.com|access-date=20 December 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120829144713/http://stats.rleague.com/rl/scorers/games/2007/200709020418.html|archive-date=29 August 2012}}</ref>

Inu played in Parramatta's 2007 preliminary final defeat by [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]] at [[Docklands Stadium]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/melbourne-v-parramatta-20091002-gdtqv2.html/|title=Melbourne v Parramatta|website=SMH|date=2 October 2009}}</ref> In August 2008, Inu was named in the [[New Zealand national rugby league team#2008 World Cup Squad|New Zealand training squad]] for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]],<ref name="Kiwis select Sonny Bill">{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_3949034,00.html|title=Kiwis select Sonny Bill|publisher=Sky Sports|date=2008-08-08|access-date=2008-08-08}}</ref> and in October 2008, he was named in the final 24-man [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#New Zealand|Kiwi]] squad that went on to win the tournament, playing one game in their win against [[Papua New Guinea national rugby league team|Papua New Guinea]].<ref name="Kiwis to wait on Webb and Matai">{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7656238.stm|title=Kiwis to wait on Webb and Matai|publisher=BBC|date=2008-10-07|access-date=2008-10-07}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2008/nov/22/new-zealand-rugby-league-world-cup|title = Rugby league World Cup final: New Zealand end Australia invincibility with 34-20 win|website = [[TheGuardian.com]]|date = 22 November 2008}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/league-the-nice-guy-finishing-first/SGKPLTIOIMZ7JG7FUUI47AJN4I/|title=League: The nice guy finishing first}}</ref>

After a 2-4 start to the [[2009 NRL season|2009 season]], Parramatta decided to re-tool their line-up, dropping Inu to the [[New South Wales Cup]], with [[Taulima Tautai]] replacing him for the round 7 match against the [[Brisbane Broncos]].<ref>
{{cite news|publisher=ABC News |title=Taylor, Inu out of Broncos-Eels clash |url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/stories/2009/04/22/2549266.html |archive-url=https://archive.today/20121231071629/http://www.abc.net.au/news/stories/2009/04/22/2549266.html |url-status=dead |archive-date=31 December 2012 |access-date=23 April 2009 |date=22 April 2009 }}</ref>  Inu played in the [[2009 NRL Grand Final]] for Parramatta against the [[Melbourne Storm]] at centre.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/league/5716278/Warriors-Krisnan-Inu-old-hand-at-grand-finals|title = Warriors' Krisnan Inu old hand at grand finals|date = October 2011}}</ref>  At the end of the 2010 season, Inu told coach [[Daniel Anderson (rugby league)|Daniel Anderson]] that he would be joining The [[New Zealand Warriors]] on a two-year deal.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.odt.co.nz/sport/league/league-warriors-sign-parramatta-pair |title=League: Warriors sign Parramatta pair |publisher=[[Otago Daily Times]] |date=11 May 2010 |access-date=4 December 2021}}</ref>

===New Zealand Warriors===
Inu played in New Zealand's 2011 NRL grand final loss to The [[Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles]].<ref name="foxsports.com.au"/> Inu's up-and-down tenure at the Warriors was curtailed in May 2012 with his release and immediate signing with the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].<ref>[http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/news/parramatta-stars-krisnan-inu-and-feleti-mateo-sign-for-new-zealand-warriors/story-e6freuy9-1225864625085 Parramatta stars Krisnan Inu and Feleti Mateo sign for New Zealand Warriors] ''Daily Telegraph'', 10 May 2010</ref>

===Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs===
Inu was released by the Warriors in May 2012 so he could immediately join the [[Des Hasler]] coached Canterbury side.<ref name=nzh2012a>{{cite web |url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2012-05-24/inu-leaves-warriors-for-bulldogs/4030940 |title=Inu leaves Warriors for Bulldogs |publisher=ABC |date=24 May 2012 |access-date=1 May 2022}}</ref>
[[File:Krisnan Inu.jpg|thumb|right|275px|Inu playing for the Bulldogs in 2014]]
He signed with the Canterbury outfit until the end of the 2015 season.<ref name=nzh2012a/>  Inu played in the [[2012 NRL Grand Final]] for Canterbury which they lost 14-4 against [[Melbourne Storm|Melbourne]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2012/09/25/bulldogs-grand-final-team-list/|title = Bulldogs' Grand Final Team List|date = 25 September 2012}}</ref>

===Catalans Dragons===
Inu returned to rugby league and was signed by [[Catalans Dragons]] in June 2015 to add depth to a depleted squad.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/10005678/krisnan-inu-signs-new-12-month-contract-with-catalans-dragons |title=Krisnan Inu signs new 12-month contract with Catalans Dragons |author=Amy Jones |publisher=Sky Sports |date=25 September 2015 |access-date=4 December 2021}}</ref>  On 17 November 2017, Inu signed a two-year deal to join The [[Widnes Vikings]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/sport/widnes-vikings-sign-krisnan-inu-13918286|title=Widnes Vikings sign Krisnan Inu from Catalans Dragons on a two-year deal|date=17 November 2017}}</ref>

===Widnes Vikings===
On 4 April 2018, Inu broke his leg in a match against [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]].  Before the injury, Inu had scored six tries in nine games.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/43647760|title = Broken leg rules out Widnes centre Inu|work = BBC Sport}}</ref>

===Salford Red Devils===
On 27 March 2019, it was announced on the Salford Red Devils official website that Inu, signed a contract with immediate effect. He joined the [[Super League]] club on a deal that would see him return to the [[Super League]] after previous stints with the [[Catalans Dragons]], and [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.salfordreddevils.net/salford-red-devils-seal-deal-for-krisnan-inu/|title=Salford Red Devils seal deal for Krisnan Inu|date=27 March 2019}}</ref>

Inu played in the [[2019 Super League Grand Final]] defeat by [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at [[Old Trafford]].<ref name="St Helens give Justin Holbrook the perfect send-off with commanding Grand Final victory over Salford">{{cite web|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/rugby-league/2019/10/12/st-helens-vs-salford-red-devils-super-league-grand-final-live/|title=St Helens give Justin Holbrook the perfect send-off with commanding Grand Final victory over Salford|publisher=Telegraph|date=12 October 2019|access-date=25 October 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2019/oct/12/st-helens-v-salford-red-devils-super-league-grand-final-live/|title=St Helens 23-6 Salford Red Devils: Super League Grand Final – as it happened|website=[[The Guardian]] |date=12 October 2019|last1=Unwin|first1=Will}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/11777123/st-helens-win-2019-super-league-league-leaders-shield/|title=St Helens win 2019 Super League League Leaders' shield|website=Sky Sports}}</ref>

On 17 October 2020, Inu played in the [[2020 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat for Salford against Leeds at [[Wembley Stadium]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/54555957|title=Leeds beat Salford 17-16 after Luke Gale lands late winning drop-goal|work=BBC Sport}}</ref>

===FC Lézignan XIII===
On 28 October 2021, it was reported that he had signed for [[FC Lézignan XIII]] in the [[Elite One Championship]]<ref>{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League|date=28 Oct 2021|accessdate=7 Nov 2021|title=Krisnan Inu finds new club|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/salford-krisnan-inu-new-club-lezignan/}}</ref>

===Leigh===
In February 2022, Inu signed a one-year deal with [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]] in the [[RFL Championship]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://uk.sports.yahoo.com/news/leigh-confirm-signing-krisnan-inu-172000704.html/|title=Leigh confirm signing of Krisnan Inu|website=uk.sports.yahoo.com}}</ref>
On 28 May 2022, Inu played for Leigh in their [[2022 RFL 1895 Cup]] final victory over [[Featherstone Rovers|Featherstone]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.yorkshirepost.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/featherstone-rovers-1895-cup-final-defeat-to-leigh-centurions-gives-brian-mcdermotts-side-signal-of-their-progress-3713313/|title=Featherstone Rovers: 1895 Cup final defeat to Leigh Centurions gives Brian McDermott's side signal of their progress|website=www.yorkshirepost.co.uk}}</ref>
On 3 July, Inu scored a [[hat-trick]] and kicked eight goals in Leigh's 56-6 victory over [[Bradford Bulls|Bradford]].  On 10 July, Inu successfully kicked all eleven conversion attempts in Leigh's 66-10 victory over [[Workington Town]]. Following Leigh's promotion to the Super League, Inu announced his retirement.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.leighjournal.co.uk/news/20269245.nene-macdonald-hat-trick-leighs-big-win-workington/|title=Nene Macdonald hat-trick in Leigh's big win at Workington|website=www.leighjournal.co.uk|date=10 July 2022 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/krisnan-inu-salford-red-devils-retire-coaching/|title=Krisnan Inu: Salford Red Devils man on tough decision to retire and his love for coaching|website=www.loverugbyleague.com|date=13 July 2023 }}</ref>

==Goal kicking style==
Inu is known for smiling and laughing before attempting a conversion at goal.  This was first revealed during the [[2007 NRL season]].  It later became his trademark and some media Sydney media outlets dubbed him "The Smiling assassin".  Inu explained his reason behind this saying "It started at training, Every time I practised kicking, the first thing I'd try to do is kick from the sideline and everyone would start bagging me out and I would start laughing.  It's the same when the crowd starts booing. I just start smiling and laughing. If they are going to boo me I might as well laugh about it and if I get it over it just shows them up so I can laugh even more".<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10495543/|title=More than reason to smile now|website=[[The New Zealand Herald]] }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.heraldsun.com.au/sport/nrl/best-and-worst-of-2007/news-story/fdb4dc7abcfa224091a0f84b6c44540c?sv=ac6736b54758066f2b5abc69bf0dffbc/|title=Best and worst of 2007|website=Herald Sun}}</ref>

==Coaching career==
===North Wales Crusaders===
On 26 March 2026 it was reported that he had taken the role of head-coach for [[North Wales Crusaders]] in the [[RFL Championship]]<ref>{{cite web|website=Love Rugby League|date=26 Mar 2026|author=Ben Olawumi|title=Krisnan Inu’s first words after landing surprise Championship head coach role|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/krisnan-inu-appointed-nw-crusaders-head-coach-march-2026}}</ref>

On 1 June 2026 the club reported that [[Mike Grady (rugby league)|Mike Grady]] had taken over the head coach role.<ref>{{cite web|website=Total RL|date=1 Jun 2026|accessdate=2 Jun 2026|author=Stephen Ibbetson|url=https://www.totalrl.com/north-wales-crusaders-appoint-mike-grady-as-head-coach/|title=North Wales Crusaders appoint Mike Grady as head coach}}</ref>
